# ForeSight 2026
<!--{ "pin": "top", "headTitle": "ForeSight 2026 — a streamlined tabletop RPG", "description": "A modern edition of the ForeSight RPG: one resolution rule (Score × Ease Factor), lethal fast combat, and a careful generative magic system." }-->

A streamlined, modern edition of the **ForeSight** tabletop RPG, built on the unpublished 2004 second edition and the 1986 original.

**One rule:** Success Chance = Score × Ease Factor — roll D100 under it. Everything else is that rule applied to different situations.

Use the navigation to browse the rules one-pagers, or open the live **[Character Builder](/character-builder/)**.

text:
# Combat
<!--{ "parent": "conflict", "order": 1 }-->

*One-pager. The whole of combat is the One Rule plus two questions: **when do you act?** (Initiative) and **how much can you do at once?** (Simultaneous Action). Everything below is those two ideas applied.*

Combat runs in **rounds** — brief bursts of violence separated by waiting for someone to make a move. There is no action-point budget, and no list of action types to memorise.

**A round is as long as the contest's tempo demands.** Direct personal combat is roughly 3–5 seconds a round, with random-length lulls between bursts (ducked behind cover, catching your breath). Slower contests run longer rounds — see Pursuit. Don't track the clock; the GM sets the tempo to fit the scene.

The round is the **quantum**: Initiative cuts time into rounds and decides who moves within one, and Simultaneous Action prices whatever you try to cram into one. The two ideas are the same idea seen from opposite ends, which is why the pair covers the whole of combat between them.

---

## Initiative — when do you act?

### Awareness: from narrative to "shit going down"

The hard, real moment is the transition out of story-telling into a fight. Every character starts that moment somewhere on a spectrum:

> **Surprised** (woken by the attack) → **Startled** → **Alert** → **Ready** → **Lying in wait** (the ambusher)

Awareness sets your **Initiative**. Coarse, obvious effects modify it: a visored helmet or a blind corner is a disadvantage; a sniper studying the scene in perfect safety is at a large advantage. Once the fight is live, awareness tends to level out to simple questions — chiefly, *can you see what's happening?*

### The Round

1. **Roll Initiative** (awareness-driven). Order everyone best to worst; ties broken by score.
2. **Declare, in order, act or wait.** Acting characters resolve their action when declared. Waiting characters hold for a trigger.
3. **Waiting persists.** Anyone still waiting from last round stays waiting until their declaration comes up again. *The totally unaware get no declaration on the first round at all — which is why ambushes are murder.*

### Reaction & Pre-emption

When a waiting character reacts to something (usually someone else acting), they may act **immediately after** it — or try to **pre-empt** and go **just before**.

> **Pre-emption = a Coordination roll, modified by your Initiative QR**, ties resolved by Initiative Score. Reflexes do the work; awareness only biases the odds — no good having lightning reflexes if you're confused. Succeed and you go just before; fail and you go just after. It's still a roll, so even a superbly coordinated, well-placed shooter keeps real uncertainty.

A pre-emption can itself be pre-empted (the counter-sniper), but you'll rarely go deeper than that.

> **You never know for certain you'll act first.** You can be pretty sure — but not certain. This is the whole game. The fast gunslinger *probably* draws first, the sniper *probably* fires before the hostage-taker reacts; neither is guaranteed. It's exactly why a police sniper in a standoff is loath to just shoot the other sniper. Remove that uncertainty and every gunfight is just "highest number wins" — which is why these scenes don't work in other games and do work here.

*E.g. an archer waits, trigger: "the mage does something." The mage starts a spell. The archer pre-empts (QR of Initiative); on success the arrow lands before the spell goes off — but a confident archer can still be a hair too slow.*

---

## Simultaneous Action — how much do you get done?

A turn's natural shape is **advance–attack–retreat**, or **pop out–fire–pull back**. How well any of it goes depends on one thing: how much of your attention it got.

> **Every task takes −1 for each *other* task you're attempting, and −1 more for each task you put *ahead* of it.**

Declare your tasks in priority order and read off the ladder:

| You are attempting | 1st | 2nd | 3rd | 4th |
|---|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| **one thing**, with total focus | 0 | | | |
| **two things** | −1 | −2 | | |
| **three things** | −2 | −3 | −4 | |
| **four things** | −3 | −4 | −5 | −6 |

Take the modifier as an **ease factor** hit (you do it *worse*) or as a hit to **yield** (you do *less* of it) — whichever the task makes sense of. Read one column, as always: fast *or* good *or* much.

### What counts as a task

**A task is a thing you want rolled.** That is the whole test, and it polices itself. A step, a shouted word, drawing a weapon, aiming — these aren't tasks; they happen, with their honest consequences (pop up to fire and you're still half-exposed when the round ends). There are still no "free actions": a thing is either of no consequence, or it isn't free. But it only costs you *attention* if you wanted it to go well enough to roll for it.

So *advance–attack–retreat* is one task — the attack — and takes no penalty. Nobody has to adjudicate anything. Decline to make your movement a task and you simply move: noisily, visibly, and at the GM's discretion.

> *A sniper on a stable platform, tracking an unaware target, doing nothing else: one thing, total focus, no modifier. A lone officer advancing through a dark basement — trying to work out where the enemy is, keep her weapon on target, **and** move quietly — is attempting three things, at −2/−3/−4, on top of a situation that was already against her. She is not going to find him. That is the correct answer; she survives that scene because he cocks his revolver, not because she rolled well.*

### Help sheds tasks

A helper doesn't hand you a bonus — they **take a task off your list**, and the ladder does the rest.

*A sniper ranging his own shot and firing is attempting two things: the shot is at −1. Give him a spotter who owns the ranging, and he is down to one thing, at 0. The spotter is worth exactly +1 — and it falls out of the rule rather than being stipulated.*

Two consequences worth having. Help can never push you past your own focused best, because the ladder bottoms out at one task — so assistance cannot be stacked into absurdity. And a helper who takes nothing gives nothing. The shed task is a real task: the spotter rolls for it, and a spotter who blows the roll hands over bad numbers.

A helper who is genuinely *better* at the task than you is a separate matter — that's good information, and it earns an ordinary situational ease-factor bonus (the same family as *aimed* +1, *braced* +1). That is where a second point comes from, not from the ladder.

### Reactions: Parry, Dodge, Flinch, Duck

Reactions are tasks too — but tasks you *didn't plan*, so they're priced differently. They are **not a pool**. Take as many as you like; each carries a **cumulative −2**. Keep parrying and you simply get worse and worse at it. Skill decides how far down that ladder you stay dangerous, so the better fighter naturally gets more good reactions — no bookkeeping required.

> **The parry you saw coming is cheaper than the parry you didn't.** Tasks you *declare together* share their cost on the ladder above; a reaction you were surprised into pays the full −2 each. This is a large part of why waiting — declaring a trigger and holding for it — is worth so much.

- **Parry** (opposed): a critical parry lets you counter-attack; an overwhelming one leaves the attacker off-balance (disarm, or trade places).
- **Dodge** (opposed): **dodge and give ground** — back up a step — for a one-off advantage. This is what makes melee *move*: fighters circle, press, and yield ground organically, instead of standing toe-to-toe.
- **Flinch / duck:** if you're exposed and someone shoots, reacting back behind cover is a legitimate reaction (at the stacking penalty).

### Never pay twice

The ladder is the price of dividing your own attention. **The situation's ease factor describes the world** — range, cover, light, the target's movement — and never your own multitasking. If shooting-on-the-move already cost you −1 for being two tasks, it does *not* also take a "firing on the move" penalty. Charge it once, on the ladder, and leave it there.

---

## Beyond Combat

This is just **initiative and attention for any tense situation**. The quick-draw, the ambushed negotiation, the scramble to grab the artifact before the other guy — awareness, act-or-wait, pre-emption, and "how many things are you trying to do at once?" resolve all of them. Reach for this page whenever timing and nerve decide the outcome, not only when weapons come out.

Note that simultaneity is measured against the **quantum**, so the same rule scales with the tempo without a special case. Moving and watching are two tasks inside a three-second burst of gunfire; across a long pursuit turn, or an afternoon of tracking, they're two things you alternate between — one task each.

*Emergent tactics: in almost every other RPG, players concentrate fire to kill one target at a time, because a dead enemy is the only less-dangerous enemy. In ForeSight a wound is −(level) to everything and a stun takes an enemy out of the turn, so a hurt, rattled foe is dramatically less dangerous than a healthy one. After dealing with the worst threats, the smart play is to **spread** attacks across as many enemies as possible — degrade the whole group rather than execute one at a time. This falls out of the rules on its own, and it happens to mirror how real firefights are won: by suppression, not by headcount.*

*Designer's Note: the gedanken experiments that drove this whole system were always the Western shootout, the same shootout with a dastardly sniper, the Iai duel, and the hostage situation. None of them resolve correctly in any other TTRPG — the nastiest edge cases of combat, that vastly more complex systems (Aftermath, I'm looking at you) never even tried to model. They cost one page here because EF and QR do the work: every ruling is "make an Initiative roll and read its QR," or "count what you're trying to do at once." Nothing to memorize, and the probability arrows all point the right way.*
title: Combat

# Equipment
<!--{ "parent": "world", "order": 1 }-->

*One-pager — the keystone. Everything you can own, drive, wield, or wear is an **object**: a basic object (a noun) plus modifications (adjectives). Weapons, armor, vehicles, and tools are just objects with extra stats. NPCs and monsters use the same pattern (see those pages).*

## Object = noun + adjectives

A **basic object** is the noun: *sword*, *pistol*, *sedan*, *backpack*. A **modification** is an adjective with a defined game effect: *fine*, *shoddy*, *sporty*, *cut-down*. You build the item you want by putting adjectives in front of the noun — and that's exactly how you say it at the table: a *cut-down pistol*, a *sporty sedan*.

This is also how the catalog stays short. If a "new" item is really a variant of one you already have, it isn't a separate entry — it's an adjective on an existing noun: a **carbine** is a *light rifle*, a **destrier** is a *warhorse*. One good adjective often covers several real items: the weapon-specific **cut-down** gives you both a sawn-off shotgun *and* a snub-nose revolver — easier to conceal, worse at range. One noun, many items.

## Stats every object can have

Most objects need only a couple of these; list a stat only when it isn't obvious.

- **PM (Performance Modifier)** — the ease-factor modifier for using the object as intended. A *+1* sword is a better sword; it does nothing extra when used to hammer nails. Some objects carry several labeled PMs (a melee weapon has Attack, Parry, and Damage).
- **Size / Weight** — for carrying, concealment, and encumbrance.
- **TL** — its technology level (and so whether a given culture can buy, repair, or *originate* it — see Technology Levels).
- **Price** — in svu.
- **Fail** — how prone it is to break or jam, if that ever matters.
- **Damage Scale** — for very large or tough objects (see Damage).

## Categories

Each category is its own short, **alphabetized** list with a few category-specific stats:

- **Weapons** — Attack, Parry, Damage (with type and penetration), Reach or Range, Usage (one/two-handed, etc.).
- **Armor** — Protection (by damage type), and the penalties it imposes (perception in a helmet, coordination in gauntlets, agility in a heavy suit).
- **Vehicles** — Cruise, Max, Terrain, Accelerate, Turn, Redline, Passengers, Cargo. **Spacecraft are vehicles**; an electric/hybrid drive is a TL6 modification, not a new category.
- **Tools** — the PM they grant to the task they're for (binoculars, lock picks, a surgeon's kit).
- **Other Stuff** — everything else, with the minimal stats it actually needs.

## Modifications (the general set)

These apply to almost anything; categories add their own.

| Mod | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Custom / Quality** | Pay a lot for one specific advantage (+1), often with a minor offset — usually reliability or weight. This is also what a character's own skill produces when they customize gear: a successful craft/tune task *makes* something Custom. |
| **Cheap / Shoddy** | Worse across the board, and especially unreliable — but much cheaper. |
| **Poorly Maintained** | Even more unreliable than Shoddy, and sometimes worse in other respects. A condition, not a price point; gear drifts here through neglect. |
| **Reliable** | Fails far less often. More expensive. |
| **Light** | Smaller, lighter, often cheaper — but a little worse, or lower capacity. |
| **Heavy** | Bigger, heavier, more expensive — higher capacity or sturdier. |

> **Grain note.** ForeSight's modifiers are **ease factors** — steps on the EF ladder (0, ¼, ½, 1, 2 … 15), applied before SC = Score × EF. A single step is a powerful lever: **+1 EF adds your whole Score to the success chance in the sweet spot** (Score 15 at EF 5→6 goes 75% → 90%, i.e. +15), and about **half your Score** down at the ¼/½ rungs. (Contrast ForePlay, where +1 was a flat ~10%, tapering at the edges.) Two consequences: modifiers stay small — a modification is usually just **±1 EF** — and the same +1 helps a more skilled character *more* in absolute terms. When porting ForePlay numbers, **its +2 or +3 maps to roughly +1 EF here**, never one-to-one.

## Descriptions: say what it is and why you'd want one

List entries get **minimal** descriptions — but useful ones. The job of a description is to tell a reader what the thing *is* and *why a character would carry it*. D&D famously listed a 10′ pole and never said it was for probing floors for traps from a safe distance; don't do that. One honest sentence beats a paragraph of stats restated in prose.

*Designer's Note: "objects are nouns plus adjectives" is the same idea that runs through NPCs, vehicles, and even background factors. Learn it once and the whole back half of the book is the same move applied to different lists. (And you already speak this language — nobody says "pistol, cut-down"; they say "cut-down pistol.")*
title: Equipment

# ForeSight — GM Cheat Sheet
<!--{ "parent": "reference", "order": 1 }-->

## The One Rule

> **Success Chance = Score × Ease Factor.** Roll **D100 ≤ SC** to succeed.

**EF ladder:** 0 — ¼ — ½ — 1 — 2 — 3 … 15. (0 = impossible, 15 = automatic.) Add all modifiers, *then* apply.

**A +1 EF is worth your whole Score** in the sweet spot (Score 15: EF 5→6 = 75%→90%), ~half at the ¼/½ rungs. Keep modifiers small.

**Rule of thumb:** `+5` trivial · `+3` very easy · `+1` easy · `0` average · `−1` hard · `−3` very hard · `−5` extreme · `−7` near-impossible. *(Just remember +5 easy / −5 hard.)*

## Quality Ratings

| Roll vs SC | QR |
|---|---|
| ≤ SC/10 | **QR1** |
| ≤ SC/4 | **QR2** |
| ≤ SC/2 | **QR3** |
| ≤ SC | **QR4** |
| > SC | **QR7** (fail) |
| > SC & a multiple of 10 | **QR10** (botch) |

*Caps: a 100 is never better than QR7; a 99 never better than QR4.*

**Yield %:** QR1 **100** · QR2 **80** · QR3 **50** · QR4 **25** (failures yield as QR4).
**Read one column only:** *Yield* (how much) · *Time* (QR4 ≈ 200% of estimate) · *PM* = **4 − QR**. Never penalize the same choice twice (pay the EF up front, the success includes it).

## Open-Ended Resolution

- **Oppose:** both roll, **compare QRs** — better wins; **equal cancels** (just-barely block; keep going); break ties by Score only if forced.
- **Block (how much got through):** defense ≥ attack negates; else attack drops **(5 − defending QR)** notches; failed defense = full; QR10 defense = attack one notch *worse*.
- **Build on a result:** **+(5 − QR)** as an EF *modifier* (QR1 +4 … QR4 +1); opposing a standing result, same as a penalty.

## Damage

> **Damage = Yield × (DC ÷ 2)** ⟶ wound levels.  (Large/tough target: **÷ Damage Scale**.)

**DC anchors:** inhumanly-strong fist **4** · 9mm pistol **8** · 7.62 rifle **11**.
**Types:** M melee · I impact · B beam. **P**n weapon pierces n DC of armor; armor **A** absorbs 1 damage.
**Hit location:** roll D10, or call the shot for an up-front EF penalty (covers cover, too).

## Combat (sequence)

1. **Awareness → Initiative.** Surprised → Startled → Alert → Ready → Lying-in-wait; modify for big factors (can't see = bad). Sets the roll.
2. **Declare best → worst Init** (ties by Score): each chooses **Act** or **Wait**. Waiting *persists* across rounds — the unaware get no declaration turn 1 (ambush!).
3. **React / pre-empt:** a waiter reacting may go just after — or **pre-empt** (Coordination roll modified by Initiative QR, ties by Init Score) to go just before. *Nobody is ever certain they act first.*
4. **Your turn:** one **Primary** + one **Secondary** action (no AP budget; throughput = skill & body).
5. **Reactions** (parry/dodge/flinch/cover): unlimited, **cumulative −2 each**.

*Round ≈ 3–5 s (longer for vehicles). Spread attacks — a hurt/stunned foe is far less dangerous than a dead-or-healthy one.*

## Wounds, Stun & Exhaustion

**Wound level** 0→8(dead); **−(level) to everything**. New damage adds on. Pain Resistance (EN *or* WP) on a hit → shrug / **Shaken** (−2, one action) / **Stunned** (only recover-from-stun; QR1 clears, QR10 KO).
**Convalescence:** roll EN/WP at the level's interval (Light daily, Med 5d, Heavy 20d, Critical+ every 30 min/5 min/min) — success drops a level, QR10 raises; worst-level penalty until healed/treated.

**Exhaustion** (fatigue, mana, etc.): hard exertion → EN/WP check, fail = **+1 level**. **Full** penalty to its own domain, **half** to all else, tracks **stack** (round half up). Clears by **rest, one level at a time** — depth 1 = breather, 2 = long break, 3 = sleep, 4 = near-coma, 5+ = intervention. 4+ converts to wounds.

**Power pools** (Mana/Piety/Spirit): gather roll → success = free · marginal/fail = +1 exhaustion · QR10 = fizzle + exhaustion. **Push it:** take a level for a one-off **+4**.
title: ForeSight — GM Cheat Sheet

text:
# Introduction
<!--{ "parent": "core", "order": 1 }-->

ForeSight is a general-purpose role-playing game. It first appeared in 1986 as a science-fiction game system, grew a fantasy and historical sibling (*HindSight & the Age of Reason*, 1988), and was substantially rewritten in 2004. This edition is a streamlining of all of that: one book, one rule, and a character you can build in five minutes.

*Designer's Note: this edition bears a very strong resemblance to a game that was typed on twenty sheets of paper in 1984–85. That's probably the result of one very good design decision: ForeSight has one rule for resolution. Nearly everything else has been simplified, consolidated, or deleted — usually by noticing that two rules were secretly the same rule, and keeping the better one.*

## What ForeSight Is

**ForeSight is about more than just combat.** A lot of role-playing games are little more than combat systems with some arm-waving to cover all other forms of human activity. ForeSight's resolution rule applies to everything — picking a lock, charming a border guard, performing surgery, shooting someone — so the rules players can depend on in a tight spot are the same rules they use everywhere else.

**ForeSight runs lean and mean.** Combat happens quickly — minutes, not hours — partly because the resolution system is easy to use from memory, and partly because weapons are lethal.

**ForeSight is plausible.** The game was designed to minimize falsehoods implied by the game system, and truths denied by it. Likelihoods of events should be believable. Ordinary people are representable, not just adventurers. Looks matter, knowledge matters, and nobody becomes an expert scuba diver by winning a Nobel prize.

**ForeSight is transparent.** Game systems should not color the underlying setting. ForeSight is adaptable *to* settings rather than grafted *onto* them: the same rules have run hard SF, swords-and-sorcery, espionage, and the Age of Reason without changing anything but the equipment lists and the available talents.

**ForeSight is the rules you can remember.** When game mechanics are used, it should be easy to apply them, and the rulebook should seldom — if ever — need to be opened at the table.

The design philosophy behind all of this is simple: **keep the mechanics simple, push the probabilities in the right general direction with the right general magnitude, and let the results speak for themselves.** ForeSight doesn't try to simulate reality precisely; it tries to make believable things likely and absurd things rare, using rules you can run from memory. Time and again that produces lifelike outcomes nobody had to write a special rule for — and that's the whole idea.

## What You Need

At least one player willing to be the gamemaster (GM), one or more other players, two ten-sided dice per person (ideally in two colors, to roll D100), copies of the character sheet, pencils, and an adventure or the makings of one.

## Darn Good Reason

Every rule in a role-playing game will be broken at some point, and many will be broken frequently. A player or GM who wants to stretch, break, or ignore a rule should give a Darn Good Reason.

That's it.

## How To Use This Book

Rather than memorizing the rules, get the gist of them. A player needs the next chapter (the One Rule) and a character — the chapter after that shows how to build one in minutes. A GM should be thoroughly familiar with Resolution and passingly familiar with the balance of the text. ForeSight is not a game for rules-lawyers — although, with them in mind, ambiguities have been minimized — but a role-playing game best played with all the rules you can remember.

*Designer's Note: if you have never played a role-playing game before: a role-playing game involves players and a gamemaster. The GM creates an imaginary setting; the players create characters who live in it. The GM describes the situation, the players say what their characters do, the outcomes are determined (usually by common sense, sometimes by the One Rule), and the GM describes the new situation. The role-playing comes in when players have their characters act according to their characters' natures rather than their own. The GM does more work, but GMs have more fun.*
title: Introduction

text:
# Magic — Applications by Fundamental
<!--{ "parent": "magic", "order": 2 }-->

*The effect catalog, carried forward from the 1987 HindSight / 2004 magic rules. Applications are listed by intensity (complexity). Provisional source for the Magic chapter rework; scaling formulas are rough OCR artifacts pending cleanup. `Z` = the intensity variable. Original cramped wording is preserved in each entry's `desc_raw` in the JSON.*

## Abeyance
*Range: infinite · Duration: see below · Props: a leash, or an hourglass*
> this is pretty nifty! Voluntarily ending an abeyance counts as an Action Point action. Optional : if an abeyed effect is "placed in" an object/substance deemed suitable by the GM, then the effect will be abeyed until the object/substance is used or consumed in the appropriate manner. If the object/substance is not utilised before the abeyance would be expended, then the effect is lost. (Eg. an application of Images, designed to produce the appearance of a door, could be placed in a container of paint. If Abeyance §2 were used, then if the paint were brushed on a section of wall within the next day, the image of the door would appear. If the paint were left for more than a day before being used, its virtue would be lost, and it would merely be paint.) The object/substance used should also have arcane components/ingredients (GM's discretion; preferably reflecting the nature of the props) added, costing at least as much as similar ingredients for conjurors.

### Intensity 0
#### Simple Abeyance (§1B1)

Delay the whole or a part of the magical effect of which this application is part by up to 30s (**fixed, bestowed**).

### Intensity 1
#### Abeyance (§1B2)

Delay the whole or a part of the magical effect of which this application is part by up to one day (**controlled** or **fixed**, **bestowed**).

*Scaling: at intensity Z'up to Z2 day(s)'*

### Intensity 2
#### Master Abeyance (§1A3)

Delay a magical effect of which this application is part indefinitely (**controlled** or **fixed**, **bestowed**).

### Intensity 3
#### External Link (§1A4)

Delay a magical effect's starting to work until another effect is incorporated into it.

The subsequently incorporated effect must be prespecified (although it can, itself, include this application), and the behaviour of the completed effect must be determined in advance.

Once a new magical effect is incorporated into an effect with an external link, the link is sealed (disappears), and the two combine to form a single magical effect, which starts functioning straight away.

This application is designed to allow a group of magicians to build complex effects, combining their knowledge and skills, or to allow a single magician to produce a difficult effect in stages. As such, it is particularly useful if the 'flashy magic' option is not in use.

(Duration: 24h.)

## Air
*Range: 40m · Duration: 2min · Props: a fan (or perhaps even an ardent admirer)*
> : the Aristotlean view of the elements implicitly assumed in these rules assumes that the element of air possesses the quality of being hot and wet, and hence equivalent effects as those below producing only the heating or wetting effect should be available at an intensity one greater (the element is more easily accessable than its qualities). I think that the qualities hot and wet might equally belong to the element of earth (volcanoes, lava, geysers, etc.), and the air instead possess the qualities cold and dry. If you wish such a view to pervade your campaign, the necessary changes shouldn't prove too troublesome. The choice is the GM's.

### Intensity 0
#### Conjure Air (§2B1)

Create a 5m×5m×5m volume of air in a space perhaps already occupied by air or vapour (but nothing denser). This volume may be decreased, but the air may not be compressed further.

Used judiciously, this spell creates gusts of wind, etc. (**immediate**).

### Intensity 1
#### Remove Air (§2B2)

Remove all the air or vapour from a 2m×2m×2m volume (**immediate**). This won't suffocate people, but it can produce thunderclaps, slam doors, etc.
#### Create Smoke (§2B3)

Create a volume of thick smoke occupying a volume 5m×5m×5m (**fixed**).
#### Stinging Vapours (§2B4)

Create a volume of irritating gas (equivalent to tear gas) occupying a volume 2m×2m×2m (**fixed**).
#### Dormitive Vapours (§2A5)

Create a volume of anaesthetic vapour occupying a volume 1m×1m×1m (**fixed**).

**Optional Teleport & Gate Rules.** Applications of Apportation marked with an asterisk (*) receive the following modifications to their intensity:

- Do not change the intensity if the origin is extremely similar in both form and function to the destination. (Eg. teleporting from one plush throne-room to another throne room of similar appearance; or opening a gate between two stone doorways of similar size and construction.)
- Add one to the intensity if the origin is similar in either shape or form to the destination. (Eg. teleporting to a throne room from a barn decorated so as to resemble it; or opening a gate between a stone doorway leading into a corridor and another opening into an alcove.)
- Add two to the intensity if the origin and destination are not radically different. (Eg. teleporting from an open field to a grassy knoll.) This also applies if the origin and destination are not very distinct locations — that is, land-marks or easily named locations. A room would be more "distinct" than a corridor, a crossroad more so than a road, a hill more so than a field.
- Add three (or more) to the intensity if the origin and destination are radically different. (Eg. teleporting from the middle of a desert to the shore of a lake — you might add four to the intensity.)

## Sandestin
*Range: Note: the sandestin is created in the invoker's current · Duration: 24h · Props: based on intensity: 0 requires the brain of a bird;*

### Intensity 0
#### Simple Sandestin (§17B1)

Place the controlled aspects of the magical effect of which this application is a part under the control of a simple program which is only able to perceive time. The program must comprise a series of simple, non-conditional, imperative clauses — eg. "move north for five seconds", "move 10 metres north", "move around in a circle slowly three times", or even "dart about randomly" — but not something like "walk to the end of the corridor" (**fixed, bestowed**).

### Intensity 1
#### Conditional Sandestin (§17B2)

As above, but the clauses may be conditional (including terms like "if … then …", "do … until …"). However, the verification of conditions must be based upon some senses bestowed upon the sandestin (either as part of it, or subsequently) or it will assume that all conditional terms are false. Eg. "if you sense magic then glow blue" (of course the sandestin would need some means to perceive magic — and know that that was what it was doing — and some means to glow blue, or it might go *beep* "SYNTAX ERROR").

### Intensity 2
#### Skilled Sandestin (§17A3)

As above, but the sandestin will also possess (up to) three skills of the invoker's choice, possessed by the invoker, whose formulae do not incorporate IN, with the same PCS and BEF as the invoker.

### Intensity 3
#### Judging Sandestin (§17A4)

As above, but the sandestin will also be able to make qualitative judgements based on its surroundings. (Eg. it could be told to "travel to Lochinvar as quickly as you can" and could make a series of decisions intended to do this; if told to "follow the most dangerous of my enemies" it could again make a value judgement, although it might not be correct.) Note that this assumes the sandestin has been conferred with senses.

The sandestin may use skills involving IN, and may be given (up to three years' worth of) fields of knowledge possessed by the invoker, instead of a skill. The sandestin may perform magical tasks if it has a source of fatigue to pay for them.

These sandestins have one major drawback, however: since they resent forced servitude, they will pervert the spirit, but not the letter, of any command they are given, whenever possible.

Note: if an invoker creates a magical effect containing an intensity 3 or 5 application of Sandestin, then his/her trained IN is reduced by one for its duration (which may be permanent). Lost IN and skill levels are restored when the effect stops (if the effect is obliterated — eg. by Instil — then the loss is permanent). If the invoker improves his/her IN with experience, then any loss he/she is currently suffering as a result of this rule becomes permanent.

### Intensity 5
#### Greater Sandestin (§17A5)

The sandestin's "personality" is similar, and its mind and skills equal, to those of the character who invoked it, and the sandestin will do exactly what the invoker instructs it to do. But it cannot invoke other magical effects using the invoker's skills unless it has some source of fatigue to pay for them (see Instil §14A9). (If it took over another magician using, say, compulsion, and hence gained control of his/her capabilities, it could force the magician to invoke magical effects.)

For every such sandestin the invoker already (currently) has in operation (ie. as part of an extant effect) he/she incurs a -1 modifier to all IN, and IN-related (using fields of knowledge, or IN-dependent skills), tasks.

Note that the intelligence of the sandestin is equal to the reduced intelligence and efficacy of the invoker, and incurs the same negative modifiers as the invoker was suffering at the time of invocation. !!!

### Intensity 6
#### Mind Transfer (§17A6)

The invoker may transfer his/her mind to another object or organism within range (retaining all knowledge and skills, although these may not be usable in the new body).

If the target is non-sapient, then the invoker controls the organism (assuming it fails to resist) completely, extinguishing its "soul". However, while in the body of a non-sapient organism, the invoker increases the intensity of any effect he/she attempts to invoke by two for incantations, and by four for rituals (on top of modifiers for lacking props); casting is unaffected.

If the target is sapient, then the personality with lower WP (the more original personality, in the case of equal WPs) is suppressed whenever the stronger is awake — except where the stronger personality attempts something greatly contrary to the interests of the weaker, in which case both make EF5 WP rolls, the better QR prevailing (a tie indicating the target's body is stunned for fifteen seconds or so, at which point another roll is made). A QR10 WP roll made in such circumstances causes the demise of the fumbling personality. A body left with no personality dies.

A magician's mind in an inanimate object which is not a source of fatigue (such as a staff or fatigue battery) won't be able to produce any effects, even by casting. Note that this application does not produce a "sandestin" as such. (Range 20m; Duration: instantaneous.)

Notes: this application requires a human brain as a prop. If transplanted into a body, this sandestin can use its mental skills, and its physical skills to a degree permitted by its new body (no problems if the new body is wholly superior). If stuck in an inanimate object, with no source of fatigue, then it will have to use casting at -2 or worse (for having no fatigue), or something.

Final notes: a sandestin may be treated as a target, or bestowee, for other applications in an effect of which it is part; it may be allocated a controlled, bestowed application and bestow it upon another target. If affected by a magical effect, and appropriately equipped, a §17A4 or §17A5 sandestin may resist said effect (they would require the Incant skill). A sandestin may be attached to a prepared physical object or creature of sufficient size (GM's discretion as to what is sufficient; only one sandestin per object or creature); if the creature or object is destroyed, then the entire effect is lost. Otherwise, a sandestin is assumed to occupy the location of its invoker at the time it was invoked. A sandestin may be assumed to react with the speed of its invoker (ie. not instantaneously) once preconditions for actions are met.
